variants.title: Level 1: Create Your First Class - Student Information
variants.description: Create a simple Student class with two basic properties: name and course. This is your first step in Object-Oriented Programming!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: A class is like a template. Just write 'String name;' and 'String course;' inside the class. Example: String myName;

variants.title: Level 1: Create a Phone Class
variants.description: Make a Phone class with brand and model properties. Think about your own phone!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Variables inside a class are called fields or properties. Just declare them like: String brand; and String model;

variants.title: Level 2: Make Your First Object - Create a Student
variants.description: Now let's create an actual student using the class you learned. It's like making a real person from a template!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: To create an object, write: Student student1 = new Student(); - This creates a new student in memory!

variants.title: Level 2: Create a Book Object
variants.description: Let's create a book object from a Book class. Think of creating your favorite textbook!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: The pattern is: ClassName variableName = new ClassName(); Try: Book myBook = new Book();

variants.title: Level 3: Give Your Object Some Data - Student Info
variants.description: Let's put actual information into your student object. Make it come alive with data!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use the dot (.) to access properties. Write: student1.name = "Juan"; and student1.course = "BSIT";

variants.title: Level 3: Set Phone Information
variants.description: Give your phone object real values. Use your actual phone's details!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use objectName.propertyName = value; Pattern: myPhone.brand = "Samsung";

variants.title: Level 4: Make Multiple Objects - Two Classmates
variants.description: Create two different students! Each will have their own unique information.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Create both objects first, then set their properties. Each student is independent of the other!

variants.title: Level 4: Create Two Coffee Orders
variants.description: Make a Coffee class and create two different coffee orders - like ordering at a café!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Each object stores its own data independently. Coffee1 won't affect coffee2!

variants.title: Level 5: Add Your First Method - Student Greeting
variants.description: Methods make objects DO things! Create a simple method that prints a greeting.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: A method is like a function inside a class. Write: void sayHello() { } and put System.out.println inside!

variants.title: Level 5: Create a Simple Calculator Method
variants.description: Make a calculator that can add two numbers. Your first useful method!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Pattern: int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } - This method takes 2 numbers and gives back the sum!

variants.title: Level 1: Constructor Magic - Auto-Initialize!
variants.description: Constructors set values when you create objects. No more manually setting each field!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Constructor is a special method with the SAME name as the class. Use 'this.name = n;' to assign!

variants.title: Level 1: Constructor for Product Class
variants.description: Create a Product with constructor. Like creating items in an online store!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: 'this' refers to the current object. this.name = n; assigns parameter n to object's name property.

variants.title: Level 2: Getters and Setters - Controlled Access
variants.description: Make fields private, then use public methods to access them safely!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Getter returns the value, Setter assigns it. Pattern: public String getName() { return name; }

variants.title: Level 2: Bank Account with Getters/Setters
variants.description: Create a simple bank account with protected balance. Safety first!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Private variables can only be changed through public methods. This is called encapsulation!

variants.title: Level 3: Methods That Calculate - Grade Calculator
variants.description: Create methods that do math and return results. Your personal calculator!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: double calculateAverage() { return (quiz1 + quiz2 + quiz3) / 3; } Simple!

variants.title: Level 3: Rectangle Area Calculator
variants.description: Calculate rectangle area using length and width. Geometry made easy!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Methods can do math! Write: double getArea() { return length * width; }

variants.title: Level 4: Complete Student Class - All Together!
variants.description: Combine everything: constructor, private fields, getters, and a calculation method!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Constructor assigns values, method calculates. Use this.name, this.midterm, this.finals!

variants.title: Level 4: Circle Class with Area and Circumference
variants.description: Create a complete Circle class. Math made fun!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use 3.14159 for pi. Area = pi * r * r, Circumference = 2 * pi * r

variants.title: Level 5: Multi-Parameter Methods - Smart Calculator
variants.description: Methods can take multiple inputs! Make a versatile calculator.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } For average: (a + b + c) / 3

variants.title: Level 5: String Manipulator with Parameters
variants.description: Create methods that work with text. String magic!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: For combine: return str1 + str2; For repeat: use a loop to concatenate the string!

variants.title: Level 1: Static Methods - Utility Class
variants.description: Create utility methods that don't need an object. Static methods belong to the class itself!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Static methods can be called without creating objects! Pattern: static int square(int n) { return n * n; }

variants.title: Level 1: String Utilities with Static Methods
variants.description: Build helpful text tools using static methods. No objects needed!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: For reverse, use a loop backwards. For countVowels, check each character. Remember to use 'static' keyword!

variants.title: Level 2: Array of Objects - Student List
variants.description: Work with multiple objects using arrays. Like managing a class roster!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Array of objects: Student[] students = new Student[3]; Then loop to find highest grade!

variants.title: Level 2: Product Inventory with Arrays
variants.description: Manage multiple products like a simple store inventory system!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Loop through array, multiply price * quantity for each, sum them up!

variants.title: Level 3: Objects Within Objects - Address in Person
variants.description: Create objects that contain other objects. Like nesting boxes!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: One object can contain another! Access nested data like: address.city

variants.title: Level 3: Car with Engine - Composition Example
variants.description: A car has an engine. Learn object composition!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Composition = "has-a" relationship. A Car HAS an Engine. Access: engine.horsepower

variants.title: Level 4: Method Overloading - Flexible Calculator
variants.description: Same method name, different parameters! Create multiple versions of the same method.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Overloading = same name, different parameters. Java knows which one to call based on arguments!

variants.title: Level 4: Overloaded Print Methods
variants.description: Create flexible display methods that work with different data types!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Each method can do something different. The compiler picks the right one automatically!

variants.title: Level 5: Mini Student Management System
variants.description: Put EVERYTHING together! Build a complete student management system.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Combine private fields, constructor, getters, calculations, and arrays. You've learned all these!

variants.title: Level 5: Simple Banking System
variants.description: Create a mini banking app with accounts and transactions!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: This is your final challenge! Use everything: encapsulation, validation, arrays, calculations!

variants.title: Level 1: Create a Simple Person Class
variants.description: Build a basic Person class to store information about people. Just like creating a form!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: A class is like a template or blueprint. Just write 'String name;' and 'int age;' inside the class. Easy!

variants.title: Level 1: Create a Book Class
variants.description: Make a Book class to represent your favorite books. Think about your textbooks!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Every book has a title and author. Declare them as String variables like: String title;

variants.title: Level 2: Make Your First Person Object
variants.description: Now create actual person objects using the class you learned! Like making real students from a form.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: To create an object, write: Person person1 = new Person(); The 'new' keyword makes a real object!

variants.title: Level 2: Create Vehicle Objects
variants.description: Make actual vehicle objects! Think about cars in a parking lot.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Same pattern as before! Vehicle car1 = new Vehicle(); Each object is independent.

variants.title: Level 3: Give Your Person Real Information
variants.description: Let's put actual data into your person object. Make them real!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use the dot (.) to access properties. Write: person.name = "Juan"; It's like filling out a form!

variants.title: Level 3: Set Product Details
variants.description: Fill in product information. Like adding items to an online store!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use item.name = "Laptop"; for text, item.price = 25000.00; for numbers, item.inStock = true; for yes/no!

variants.title: Level 4: Make Your Person Say Hello!
variants.description: Add a method so your person can introduce themselves. Methods make objects DO things!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Methods are like actions. Write: void introduce() { } and put System.out.println inside with name and age!

variants.title: Level 4: Calculator with Two Operations
variants.description: Make a calculator that can add AND multiply. Two simple methods!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: For add: int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } For multiply: int multiply(int a, int b) { return a * b; }

variants.title: Level 5: Simple Calculator - Add and Multiply
variants.description: Create a calculator with methods that take numbers and return results. Math made easy!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Pattern is: int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } Super simple math!

variants.title: Level 5: Circle Area Calculator
variants.description: Make a method that calculates circle area. Math + programming!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Formula is: Area = 3.14 * radius * radius. Write: double calculateArea(double r) { return 3.14 * r * r; }

variants.title: Level 1: Constructors (Account Setup)
variants.description: Learn how constructors initialize objects when they're created.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Constructors ensure objects are properly initialized. They have the same name as the class and no return type.

variants.title: Level 1: Constructor - Employee Setup Made Easy
variants.description: Create an Employee class that auto-fills name, ID, and salary!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Constructors can take parameters to set initial values. Getters and setters provide controlled access to private fields.

variants.title: Level 2: Data Validation - Smart Student Records
variants.description: Protect your data! Don't allow invalid ages or GPAs to be stored.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Encapsulation protects data by validating inputs in setter methods. Don't allow invalid values to be stored.

variants.title: Level 2: Temperature Validator - Science is Cool!
variants.description: Create a temperature class that prevents impossible temperatures (below absolute zero!).
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Physical laws provide natural validation rules. Temperature cannot go below absolute zero.

variants.title: Level 3: Multiple Classes - Mini Library System
variants.description: Build THREE classes that work together! Books, Members, and Library.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Composition means objects contain other objects. A library contains books and members that interact.

variants.title: Level 3: E-Commerce Mini System - Products & Orders
variants.description: Build an online shopping system with Product, Customer, and Order classes!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Real-world systems involve multiple objects working together. Focus on the relationships between entities.

variants.title: Level 4: Static Utility Class - String Helpers!
variants.description: Create helper methods that work WITHOUT creating objects. Super useful!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Utility classes provide functionality without needing object state. Make constructor private and all methods static.

variants.title: Level 4: Static ID Generator - Unique Numbers!
variants.description: Create a class that generates unique IDs for all students. One number tracker for everyone!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Static fields are shared across all instances. Perfect for generating unique IDs across the entire application.

variants.title: Level 5: Complete Shopping Cart - Like Shopee/Lazada!
variants.description: Build a real shopping cart system! Add items, calculate total, apply discounts.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Complex systems require careful design. Think about how items relate to the cart and what operations users need.

variants.title: Level 5: School Grade Management System
variants.description: Create a grade tracker like what teachers use! Assignments, scores, percentages.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Educational systems involve relationships between assignments, grades, and students. Model these carefully.

variants.title: Level 1: Observer Pattern - Event Notification System
variants.description: Build a notification system where observers get notified when events happen! Like YouTube subscriptions!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Observer pattern enables loose coupling. Objects can be notified of changes without tight dependencies.

variants.title: Level 1: Strategy Pattern - Swappable Sorting!
variants.description: Choose which sorting algorithm to use at runtime! Bubble Sort or Quick Sort!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Strategy pattern allows algorithms to be selected at runtime. Encapsulate each algorithm in its own class.

variants.title: Level 2: Build Your Own ArrayList<T> - Type-Safe Collection!
variants.description: Create a dynamic array like ArrayList! It grows automatically when full!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Generic collections provide type safety. Handle array resizing when capacity is exceeded.

variants.title: Level 2: Build Your Own HashMap<K,V> - Key-Value Storage!
variants.description: Create a hash map that stores key-value pairs! Like a mini dictionary!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Hash maps use arrays and linked lists for collision handling. Implement a simple hash function.

variants.title: Level 3: Decorator Pattern (Coffee Shop)
variants.description: Implement decorator pattern for a flexible coffee ordering system.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Decorator pattern adds behavior dynamically. Each decorator wraps the original object and adds features.

variants.title: Level 3: Factory Method - Shape Creator!
variants.description: Let subclasses decide which shape to create! CircleFactory creates circles, RectangleFactory creates rectangles!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Factory method delegates object creation to subclasses. Each factory creates a specific type of object.

variants.title: Level 4: Simple Singleton (Database Config)
variants.description: Create a basic singleton pattern for application configuration that ensures only one instance exists.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Singleton ensures only one instance exists. Private constructor prevents direct instantiation.

variants.title: Level 4: Simple Builder Pattern (Pizza Order)
variants.description: Create a simple builder pattern for creating pizza orders with various toppings.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Builder pattern makes object creation flexible. Each method returns 'this' to enable method chaining.

variants.title: Level 5: Student Record Manager (ArrayList Practice)
variants.description: Create a student record management system using ArrayList to store and manage multiple students.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: ArrayList makes it easy to manage collections of objects. Use loops to search and calculate statistics.

variants.title: Level 5: Simple Form Validator (Manual Validation)
variants.description: Create a form validation class that validates user input using simple methods and conditions.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Validation checks if data meets requirements. Add error messages to the errors list when validation fails.

variants.title: Level 1: Understanding Private (Keep Secrets Safe)
variants.description: Learn why we use 'private' to protect data. Like keeping your password secret!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Just add 'private' before your variables. It's like locking a door - only special methods can open it!

variants.title: Level 1: Bank Account Privacy
variants.description: Protect bank account data with private! Money should be secure.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Write 'private double balance;' to keep money info secure. Then create getBalance() to check it!

variants.title: Level 2: Getter Methods (Reading Private Data)
variants.description: Create getter methods to safely read private information. Like showing your ID card!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Getter pattern: public String getName() { return name; } Easy! Just return the private variable.

variants.title: Level 2: Product Getters
variants.description: Create getters for product information. Read-only access!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: public double getPrice() { return price; } That's it! Return the private variable.

variants.title: Level 3: Setter Methods (Changing Private Data)
variants.description: Create setter methods to safely change private information. Like updating your profile!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Setter pattern: public void setName(String n) { name = n; } or use: this.name = n;

variants.title: Level 3: Car Setters
variants.description: Add setters to change car properties. Like customizing your car!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: public void setBrand(String b) { brand = b; } Simple assignment inside the method!

variants.title: Level 4: Complete Encapsulation (Student Class)
variants.description: Put it all together! Private fields + getters + setters = Encapsulation!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: For each private field, create a getter (returns value) and setter (changes value). Easy pattern!

variants.title: Level 4: Rectangle with Area
variants.description: Complete encapsulation with a rectangle that calculates its own area!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: For getArea: public double getArea() { return width * height; } Use the private fields!

variants.title: Level 5: Simple Validation (Age Checker)
variants.description: Add basic validation to setters. Don't allow impossible ages!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: In setAge, check: if (a >= 1 && a <= 120) { age = a; } Don't set age if it's invalid!

variants.title: Level 5: Data Validation (Price Controller)
variants.description: Implement validation for a product pricing system.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Business rules can be enforced through validation. Prices should be positive, names should not be empty.

variants.title: Level 1: Smart Email Validator - Like Facebook Login!
variants.description: Validate emails and passwords just like real login systems do!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Real-world validation involves checking formats and business rules. Use string methods to validate email format.

variants.title: Level 1: Temperature Converter - Science Class!
variants.description: Build a temperature class that converts between Celsius, Fahrenheit, and Kelvin!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Physical constants provide natural validation boundaries. Temperature cannot go below absolute zero.

variants.title: Level 2: Immutable Point - Can't Change Coordinates!
variants.description: Create a point that NEVER changes. Once created, it's permanent! (Like a permanent marker)
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Immutable objects use final fields and provide no setters. Operations return new instances instead of modifying existing ones.

variants.title: Level 2: Immutable Money Class - Bank-Safe!
variants.description: Money that can't be changed accidentally. Perfect for financial apps!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Financial objects should be immutable to prevent accidental modification. Operations create new instances with calculated values.

variants.title: Level 3: Defensive Copying - Protect Your Lists!
variants.description: Make a safe copy of arrays/lists so outsiders can't mess with your data!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Defensive copying prevents external code from modifying internal collections. Always copy collections when passing them in or out.

variants.title: Level 3: Safe Contact List - No Hackers!
variants.description: Create a contact list that's protected from external tampering!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Contact lists are sensitive data. Protect them from external modification while allowing controlled access to functionality.

variants.title: Level 4: Smart Bank Account - Multiple Security Checks!
variants.description: Build a bank account with daily limits, lock status, and balance validation!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Banking systems need multiple layers of validation. Check account status, transaction limits, and available balance.

variants.title: Level 4: Document Security - Role-Based Access!
variants.description: Only admins and editors can modify! Public users can only read.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Document security requires checking user permissions before allowing operations. Track who makes changes for audit purposes.

variants.title: Level 5: Package-Private - Classes Working Together!
variants.description: Student and Teacher classes can share data because they're in the same package!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Package-private access allows classes in the same package to collaborate while hiding details from external packages.

variants.title: Level 5: Library Teamwork - Books & Library Together!
variants.description: Library can directly access Book's checkout status because they're teammates (same package)!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Library and Book classes need to work together closely. Use package-private access for internal operations.

variants.title: Level 1: Lazy Loading - Create When Needed!
variants.description: Don't create expensive resources until you actually need them! Save memory and time!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Lazy loading delays expensive operations until needed. Combine with singleton pattern for resource management.

variants.title: Level 1: Fluent Interface - Method Chaining!
variants.description: Chain methods together like: query.from("users").where("age > 18").orderBy("name").build()!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Fluent interfaces allow method chaining while maintaining encapsulation. Validate state before building final result.

variants.title: Level 2: Generic Container<T> - Type-Safe Storage!
variants.description: Create a container that stores ANY type, but maintains type safety!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Generic containers need runtime type information and bounds checking. Use type tokens to maintain type safety.

variants.title: Level 2: Smart Event System<T> - Type-Safe Notifications!
variants.description: Create an event system that only accepts the correct event types!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Event systems need careful type handling and concurrency protection. Use bounded wildcards for flexibility.

variants.title: Level 3: Smart App Config - Validate Everything!
variants.description: Create a configuration system that prevents invalid settings!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Configuration classes need validation to ensure settings are valid. Check conditions before setting values.

variants.title: Level 3: User Registration Validator
variants.description: Create a user registration system with comprehensive validation for all fields.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: User registration needs multiple validation rules. Store error messages in a list to show users what needs fixing.

variants.title: Level 4: Simple Access Control (File Manager)
variants.description: Create a file manager with user role-based access control using simple if conditions.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Access control checks user roles before allowing operations. Different roles have different permissions.

variants.title: Level 4: Activity Logger (Simple Wrapper)
variants.description: Create a logger class that wraps operations and tracks activity history.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Wrapper classes add functionality to existing objects. Log the operation before calling the wrapped object's method.

variants.title: Level 5: Configuration Manager (HashMap Practice)
variants.description: Create a configuration manager that stores and validates different types of settings using HashMap.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: HashMap allows storing key-value pairs. Use separate HashMaps for different data types to maintain type safety.

variants.title: Level 5: Website Analytics Tracker
variants.description: Create a simple analytics system that tracks page views and generates reports.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Analytics systems track events and calculate statistics. Use ArrayList to store visits and loops to count and analyze.

variants.title: Level 1: Your First Inheritance (Animal Family)
variants.description: Learn to extend classes! A Dog IS AN Animal, so it inherits animal features.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Write: class Dog extends Animal { } The magic word is 'extends'! Dog now has everything Animal has.

variants.title: Level 1: Basic Inheritance (Vehicle Hierarchy)
variants.description: Practice inheritance with a vehicle classification system.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Inheritance creates an 'is-a' relationship. A Car 'is-a' Vehicle, so it inherits all Vehicle properties and behaviors.

variants.title: Level 2: Calling Parent Constructor (Person to Student)
variants.description: Learn to use super() to initialize parent class. Every student IS A person!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: In Student constructor write: super(n, a); on first line. This calls Person's constructor!

variants.title: Level 2: Constructor Inheritance (Shape Hierarchy)
variants.description: Practice constructor chaining with geometric shapes.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Initialize parent class properties first with super(), then initialize child-specific properties.

variants.title: Level 3: Override Methods (Dog Sounds)
variants.description: Change parent method behavior! Each animal makes different sounds.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Write @Override then copy the method signature. Change what's inside. Dogs bark, not just make sound!

variants.title: Level 3: Method Overriding (Bird Classification)
variants.description: Override methods to create specialized bird behaviors.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Each bird species has unique flight patterns and sounds. Override methods to reflect these differences.

variants.title: Level 4: Using super to Call Parent (Level Up)
variants.description: Call parent method PLUS add extra stuff! Best of both worlds.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: In your levelUp: super.levelUp(); (do parent's work), then add your own code after!

variants.title: Level 4: Super Method Calls (Game Characters)
variants.description: Use super calls to enhance character abilities in a game system.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Warriors get standard level-up benefits plus extra strength. Call super.levelUp() first, then add warrior-specific bonuses.

variants.title: Level 5: Protected Access (Library Books)
variants.description: Learn 'protected' - it's like private, but child classes can see it!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Protected fields can be used directly in child classes! Just write: title (no this needed).

variants.title: Level 5: Protected Access (Media Player)
variants.description: Use protected access in a media player hierarchy for different file types.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Video players need access to basic media controls but add video-specific features like resolution.

variants.title: Level 1: Multi-Level Inheritance - Company Hierarchy!
variants.description: Build a chain: Person → Employee → Manager. Like a real company structure!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Multi-level inheritance creates a chain: Person → Employee → Manager. Each level adds more specific properties.

variants.title: Level 1: Transportation Chain - Vehicle → Motor → Motorcycle!
variants.description: Build a 3-level inheritance: Vehicle → MotorVehicle → Motorcycle!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Motorcycles inherit from MotorVehicle which inherits from Vehicle. Each level adds specialized features.

variants.title: Level 2: Abstract Classes - Shape Calculator!
variants.description: Abstract classes are templates - you can't create them directly, but children must implement them!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Abstract classes can't be instantiated but provide a template for child classes. Abstract methods must be implemented by children.

variants.title: Level 2: Abstract Game Entities - Player & Enemy!
variants.description: Create an abstract template for all game characters. Each must implement update()!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Game entities share common properties but have different update behaviors. Use abstract class to enforce implementation.

variants.title: Level 3: Multiple Interfaces - Payment System!
variants.description: One class can implement MULTIPLE interfaces! Like having multiple abilities!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Classes can implement multiple interfaces. Each interface defines a contract that must be fulfilled.

variants.title: Level 3: Triple Interfaces - File Processor!
variants.description: Implement THREE interfaces at once! Read, Write, AND Compress!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: A class can implement multiple interfaces, providing all the behaviors they define. Use commas to separate interfaces.

variants.title: Level 4: 'final' Keyword - Lock It Down!
variants.description: 'final' = can't be changed! Final classes can't be extended, final methods can't be overridden!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Final classes cannot be extended, final methods cannot be overridden. Use for security-critical code.

variants.title: Level 4: Final Config - Some Settings Can't Change!
variants.description: System settings that are final = locked forever. Custom settings = can override!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Configuration systems need some immutable settings (final) while allowing customization in other areas.

variants.title: Level 5: Composition (HAS-A) - Swap Engines!
variants.description: Composition = HAS-A. Car HAS-A Engine. Can swap engines anytime! More flexible than inheritance!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Composition (HAS-A) is often better than inheritance (IS-A) for flexibility. Objects can change their components at runtime.

variants.title: Level 5: Strategy Pattern - Change Payment Method!
variants.description: Switch payment methods at runtime! Credit card → PayPal → GCash! Super flexible!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Strategy pattern uses composition to change behavior at runtime. Much more flexible than inheritance hierarchies.

variants.title: Level 1: Make Your Own ArrayList<T> - Generic Collection!
variants.description: Build your own ArrayList that works with ANY type! Just like the real ArrayList!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Generic inheritance allows type-safe collections. Child classes inherit generic type parameters from parent.

variants.title: Level 1: Smart Cache<K,V> - Different Storage Strategies!
variants.description: Build caches that store data differently - FIFO (First In First Out) vs LRU (Least Recently Used)!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Different cache implementations share common interface but have different eviction strategies. Use composition for tracking access patterns.

variants.title: Level 2: Fluent Builder Pattern - Chain Methods Together!
variants.description: Build objects by chaining methods: new UserBuilder().setName("Juan").setEmail(...).build()!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Covariant return types allow method chaining in inheritance hierarchies. Each subclass returns its own type.

variants.title: Level 2: Vehicle Factory - Create Objects Smartly!
variants.description: Factories create objects for you! CarFactory creates Cars, MotorcycleFactory creates Motorcycles!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Factories can return more specific types than their parent class declares. This provides type safety at compile time.

variants.title: Level 3: Duck - Flies AND Swims! Multiple Abilities!
variants.description: Make a Duck that can both fly and swim by implementing multiple interfaces!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Default methods in interfaces provide multiple inheritance of behavior. Classes can implement multiple interfaces safely.

variants.title: Level 3: Smart Plugins - Configurable AND Monitorable!
variants.description: Create plugins that can be configured AND monitored! Two abilities in one!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Plugin architectures benefit from multiple interface implementation. Each interface provides a different aspect of functionality.

variants.title: Level 4: Simple Expression Evaluator (Inheritance Chain)
variants.description: Create a simple math expression evaluator using inheritance for different expression types.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Expressions can be nested. Each expression type knows how to evaluate itself and its children.

variants.title: Level 4: RPG Character Progression System
variants.description: Create a role-playing game character system with different character classes and level-up mechanics.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Template method defines common behavior (levelUp) while letting subclasses customize specific parts (applyClassBonus).

variants.title: Level 5: Coffee Shop Decorator (Add-ons System)
variants.description: Create a coffee ordering system where you can add multiple toppings using the decorator pattern.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Decorator pattern wraps objects to add functionality. Each decorator adds its cost to the wrapped coffee's cost.

variants.title: Level 5: Game Character Factory System
variants.description: Create a factory pattern system for creating different types of game characters with their equipment.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Factory pattern creates objects without specifying exact class. Each character type starts with different equipment.

variants.title: Level 1: Polymorphism Magic (Animal Sounds)
variants.description: One method name, many behaviors! Each animal makes its own sound.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Same method name (makeSound), different behaviors! Dog says Woof, Cat says Meow. Magic!

variants.title: Level 1: Basic Polymorphism (Vehicle Movement)
variants.description: Practice polymorphism with different types of vehicles and their movement methods.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use an array of Vehicle references to store different vehicle types. Each will move in their own way when move() is called.

variants.title: Level 2: Method Overloading (Calculator Tricks)
variants.description: Same method name, different parameters! Java picks the right one automatically.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Same name (add), different parameters! Java knows which one to use based on what you pass in.

variants.title: Level 2: Method Overloading (Shape Area Calculator)
variants.description: Create overloaded methods to calculate areas of different shapes.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Different shapes need different parameters for area calculation. Use method overloading to provide convenient methods for each shape.

variants.title: Level 3: Interfaces (Drawable Shapes)
variants.description: Interfaces are like contracts - everyone who signs must follow the rules!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Write 'class Circle implements Drawable' then you MUST create the draw() method. It's the contract!

variants.title: Level 3: Interface Polymorphism (Media Player)
variants.description: Create a media player system using interfaces for different file types.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Media players for different formats can all implement the same interface, allowing uniform control regardless of file type.

variants.title: Level 4: Abstract Classes (Shape Areas)
variants.description: Abstract = incomplete! Children MUST complete it.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Abstract methods have no { }. Children MUST implement them. Each shape calculates area differently!

variants.title: Level 4: Abstract Class Polymorphism (Game Characters)
variants.description: Create a game character system with different types of characters and abilities.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Game characters share common properties (health, level) but have unique combat styles. Use polymorphism for flexible battle systems.

variants.title: Level 5: Polymorphic ArrayList (Pet Shop)
variants.description: Store different animals in ONE list! That's the power of polymorphism.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: ArrayList<Animal> animals = new ArrayList<>(); Can hold ANY animal! Loop and call makeSound() - each responds differently!

variants.title: Level 5: Polymorphic Collections (Banking System)
variants.description: Create a banking system with different account types stored in collections.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Different account types have different rules for withdrawals and interest. Store them in a collection and process them polymorphically.

variants.title: Level 1: Dynamic Method Dispatch - Smart Pricing!
variants.description: Same method name, DIFFERENT results! Electronics add warranty, Clothing add size fee!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Even with Product references, the actual method called depends on the real object type. This is dynamic method dispatch in action.

variants.title: Level 1: Fare Calculator - Jeepney vs Taxi!
variants.description: Calculate fares polymorphically! Each transport type has different pricing!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: The fare calculation method called depends on the actual transport type, not the reference type. This enables flexible pricing systems.

variants.title: Level 2: 'instanceof' - Check Before You Cast!
variants.description: Use instanceof to safely check object types before performing special operations!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use instanceof to check object types before casting. This allows type-specific operations while maintaining polymorphic design.

variants.title: Level 2: Polymorphism with instanceof (Vehicle Inspector)
variants.description: Create a vehicle inspection system that handles different vehicle types safely.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Different vehicles need different inspections. Use instanceof to safely perform vehicle-specific checks while maintaining polymorphic processing.

variants.title: Level 3: Factory Pattern - Shape Generator!
variants.description: Factory creates shapes for you! Just tell it what shape you want!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Factory pattern creates objects polymorphically. Client code works with Shape interface without knowing specific implementations.

variants.title: Level 3: Database Factory - Connect to Any DB!
variants.description: Factory creates MySQL, PostgreSQL, or SQLite connections! Switch databases easily!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Database factory allows switching between different database types without changing client code. All connections implement the same interface.

variants.title: Level 4: Observer Pattern - News Subscribers!
variants.description: When news is published, ALL subscribers get notified! Email, SMS, everyone!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Observer pattern uses polymorphism to notify different types of subscribers without knowing their specific implementation.

variants.title: Level 4: Strategy Pattern - Flexible Discounts!
variants.description: Switch discount strategies! Regular customer 5%, Premium 15%, Sale 30%!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Strategy pattern allows changing algorithms at runtime. Different discount strategies can be applied polymorphically to the same shopping cart.

variants.title: Level 5: Polymorphic Calculator - +, -, *, ÷!
variants.description: Build a calculator where each operation is a separate class! Clean and organized!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Calculator operations can be implemented polymorphically. Each operation validates inputs differently and performs different calculations.

variants.title: Level 5: Form Validator - Email, Phone, Password!
variants.description: Validate different form fields polymorphically! Each has different rules!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Different fields need different validation rules. Use polymorphic validators to handle various field types with a common interface.

variants.title: Level 1: Plugin System - Load Features Dynamically!
variants.description: Build a system where you can add new features (plugins) without changing the main code!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Plugin systems use polymorphism to load and execute different functionality dynamically. Each plugin implements the same interface.

variants.title: Level 1: Request Handler Chain - Process Step by Step!
variants.description: Process web requests through multiple handlers: Auth → Logging → Main Handler!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Middleware chains process requests through multiple handlers polymorphically. Each middleware can modify the request or stop the chain.

variants.title: Level 2: Data Repository<T> - Save Any Type!
variants.description: Build a repository that can store Users, Products, or ANY type of object!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Generic repositories provide type-safe data access. Different entity types can use the same repository interface polymorphically.

variants.title: Level 2: Event System<T> - Notify & React!
variants.description: Build an event bus where different events trigger different handlers!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Generic event systems provide type safety while allowing polymorphic event handling. Each handler processes specific event types.

variants.title: Level 3: Operation Logger - Wrap & Track!
variants.description: Wrap any operation with logging to track when it runs!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Wrapper classes can add logging to any operation without changing the operation itself. Use composition and delegation.

variants.title: Level 3: Smart Form Validator - Check All Fields!
variants.description: Validate form fields polymorphically! Each field has its own validation rules!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Polymorphic validators allow different field types to be validated uniformly. Each validator knows its own rules.

variants.title: Level 4: Simple Command Pattern (Remote Control)
variants.description: Create a remote control system using command pattern for different devices.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Command pattern encapsulates actions as objects. Remote control doesn't need to know what each button does.

variants.title: Level 4: Traffic Light State System
variants.description: Create a traffic light system using polymorphism to represent different states.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: State pattern allows objects to change behavior when state changes. Each state knows what comes next.

variants.title: Level 5: Simple Observer Pattern (Weather Station)
variants.description: Create a weather monitoring system using observer pattern with multiple displays.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Observer pattern notifies multiple objects when data changes. All observers get updated automatically.

variants.title: Level 5: Payment Processing System (Strategy + Factory)
variants.description: Create a payment system combining strategy pattern and factory pattern for different payment methods.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Combine strategy pattern (different payment methods) with factory pattern (creating payment objects). Cart can use any payment method.

variants.title: Level 1: Create Your First Button
variants.description: Create a simple JFrame window with a button. This is your first GUI application!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Create a button with: JButton button = new JButton("Click Me!"); Then add it to the frame with frame.add(button);

variants.title: Level 1: Create a Window with a Label
variants.description: Make a window that displays text using JLabel. Simple but powerful!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: JLabel displays text or images. Create it like: JLabel label = new JLabel("Your text here");

variants.title: Level 1: Add a Text Field
variants.description: Create a window with a text field where users can type. Your first input component!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: JTextField lets users type text. Create it with: JTextField textField = new JTextField(20);

variants.title: Level 1: Create a Checkbox
variants.description: Add a checkbox to your window. Learn about yes/no components!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: JCheckBox creates a checkbox. Use: JCheckBox checkbox = new JCheckBox("I agree to terms");

variants.title: Level 1: Add Radio Buttons
variants.description: Create radio buttons for single-choice selection. Like choosing your favorite!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Radio buttons allow one selection. Group them with ButtonGroup so only one can be selected!

variants.title: Level 2: Button Click Counter
variants.description: Create a button that counts how many times it's been clicked. Your first interactive program!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use button.addActionListener(e -> { count++; button.setText("Clicks: " + count); }); to handle clicks!

variants.title: Level 2: Change Label on Button Click
variants.description: Make a button that changes a label's text when clicked. See events in action!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Add listener like: button.addActionListener(e -> label.setText("Text Changed!"));

variants.title: Level 2: Toggle Button Text
variants.description: Create a button that switches between two texts when clicked. Learn about state!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Toggle with isOn = !isOn; then use if (isOn) button.setText("ON"); else button.setText("OFF");

variants.title: Level 2: Button Color Changer
variants.description: Make a button that changes its own color when clicked. Fun visual feedback!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use button.setBackground(Color.BLUE); to change the button's color!

variants.title: Level 2: Show/Hide Label
variants.description: Create a button that makes a label appear and disappear. Toggle visibility!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Toggle visibility with label.setVisible(!label.isVisible()); - this makes it appear/disappear!

variants.title: Level 3: Get User Input from TextField
variants.description: Read text from a text field when button is clicked. Learn to get user input!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Get input with String text = textField.getText(); then show it with display.setText("You typed: " + text);

variants.title: Level 3: Simple Calculator - Add Two Numbers
variants.description: Create a calculator that adds two numbers from text fields!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Convert text to number with Integer.parseInt(), add them, then display with result.setText("Result: " + sum);

variants.title: Level 3: Password Field Demo
variants.description: Use JPasswordField to hide password input. Security basics!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Get password with new String(passField.getPassword()); - getPassword() returns char array for security!

variants.title: Level 3: CheckBox State Reader
variants.description: Check if a checkbox is selected or not. Learn state management!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use if(checkbox.isSelected()) to check state. Returns true if checked, false if not!

variants.title: Level 3: ComboBox Selection Reader
variants.description: Get the selected item from a dropdown (JComboBox). Choice selection!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Get selected item with String selected = (String) combo.getSelectedItem(); then display it!

variants.title: Level 4: Multiple Buttons - Different Actions
variants.description: Create multiple buttons that do different things. Learn to handle many events!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Each button can have its own separate ActionListener with different actions!

variants.title: Level 4: Label + TextField + Button Combo
variants.description: Combine label, text field, and button to create a greeting app!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Get name, then set greeting.setText("Hello, " + name + "!"); to show personalized greeting!

variants.title: Level 4: Calculator with Clear Button
variants.description: Add a clear button to your calculator. Multiple button management!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Clear button should set all text fields to empty string: num1.setText(""); and clear result too!

variants.title: Level 4: Color Picker with Buttons
variants.description: Create buttons that change a panel's color. Visual feedback!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Each button changes panel color: colorPanel.setBackground(Color.RED); for red button!

variants.title: Level 4: Simple Form with Multiple Inputs
variants.description: Create a form with multiple fields and a submit button!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Get both values, then combine them: display.setText("Name: " + name + ", Age: " + age);

variants.title: Level 5: Simple Counter App
variants.description: Create a counter with increment, decrement, and reset buttons!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Each button modifies counter variable (counter++, counter--, counter=0) then updates display!

variants.title: Level 5: Traffic Light Simulator
variants.description: Create a traffic light that cycles through RED → YELLOW → GREEN!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use if-else: if RED change to YELLOW, if YELLOW change to GREEN, if GREEN change to RED!

variants.title: Level 5: Simple Login Form
variants.description: Create a login form that checks username and password!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use .equals() to compare strings. Set color with message.setForeground(Color.GREEN) or Color.RED!

variants.title: Level 5: Calculator with 4 Operations
variants.description: Build a calculator with add, subtract, multiply, and divide!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Each button gets numbers, performs operation, displays result. Check if num2 is zero for division!

variants.title: Level 5: Temperature Converter (Both Ways)
variants.description: Convert Celsius to Fahrenheit AND Fahrenheit to Celsius!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Formula C→F: (C × 9/5) + 32, Formula F→C: (F - 32) × 5/9. Use String.format("%.2f", value)!

variants.title: Level 1: Lambda Expression Event Handlers
variants.description: Use modern lambda expressions to handle events. Clean and concise code!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Lambda syntax is simple: button.addActionListener(e -> label.setText("Clicked!"));

variants.title: Level 1: Mouse Click Coordinates Display
variants.description: Display where the user clicked on the window. Learn MouseListener!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use MouseAdapter: frame.addMouseListener(new MouseAdapter() { public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) { ... } });

variants.title: Level 1: Keyboard Key Press Detector
variants.description: Detect which key the user presses. Learn KeyListener!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use KeyAdapter: frame.addKeyListener(new KeyAdapter() { public void keyPressed(KeyEvent e) { ... } });

variants.title: Level 1: ItemListener for CheckBox
variants.description: Detect when checkbox state changes. Learn ItemListener!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: checkbox.addItemListener(e -> { if (e.getStateChange() == ItemEvent.SELECTED) ... });

variants.title: Level 1: ComboBox Selection Event
variants.description: Detect when user selects an item from dropdown. Real-time feedback!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: combo.addActionListener(e -> display.setText("Selected: " + combo.getSelectedItem()));

variants.title: Level 2: Mouse Hover Effects
variants.description: Change button appearance when mouse hovers over it. Interactive UI!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use MouseAdapter and override mouseEntered() and mouseExited() methods!

variants.title: Level 2: Focus Events on Text Fields
variants.description: Show help text when user focuses on different fields. Smart forms!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use FocusAdapter: nameField.addFocusListener(new FocusAdapter() { public void focusGained(FocusEvent e) { ... } });

variants.title: Level 2: Window Events Logger
variants.description: Log all window events (open, close, minimize, maximize). Event tracking!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use WindowAdapter and override the window event methods you need!

variants.title: Level 2: Mouse Motion Tracker
variants.description: Track mouse movement and display coordinates in real-time!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use MouseMotionAdapter: frame.addMouseMotionListener(new MouseMotionAdapter() { public void mouseMoved(MouseEvent e) { ... } });

variants.title: Level 2: Button Enable/Disable Controller
variants.description: Enable button only when checkbox is checked. Form validation basics!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Check state with e.getStateChange() == ItemEvent.SELECTED, then use setEnabled(true/false)!

variants.title: Level 3: Simple Drawing App with Mouse Drag
variants.description: Draw by dragging the mouse! Learn mouseDragged event.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: In mouseDragged, add new Point(e.getX(), e.getY()) to ArrayList and call repaint()!

variants.title: Level 3: Drag and Drop Component
variants.description: Make a label draggable around the window! Advanced mouse events.
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: On mousePressed, store click position. On mouseDragged, calculate new position and update with setLocation()!

variants.title: Level 3: Multi-Button Event Handler Pattern
variants.description: Handle multiple buttons with one ActionListener. Efficient event handling!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: One handler for all buttons! Use e.getSource() to identify which button was clicked!

variants.title: Level 3: Real-time Input Validator
variants.description: Validate input as user types. Use DocumentListener or KeyListener!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use try-catch with Integer.parseInt(), check range, then set feedback color!

variants.title: Level 3: Custom Color Mixer
variants.description: Mix colors using three sliders (R, G, B). See colors change in real-time!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Add ChangeListener to sliders: slider.addChangeListener(e -> { int r = redSlider.getValue(); ... });

variants.title: Level 4: Complete Login System with Validation
variants.description: Build a login system with real-time validation and feedback!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Validate on focus lost or key release. Check length, show GREEN for valid, RED for invalid!

variants.title: Level 4: Calculator with Memory Functions
variants.description: Build a calculator with M+, M-, MR, MC memory functions!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: M+ adds to memory, M- subtracts, MR recalls, MC clears. Update status label after each operation!

variants.title: Level 4: Text Editor with Character Counter
variants.description: Build a simple text editor that counts characters in real-time!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: textArea.getDocument().addDocumentListener() for real-time updates. Split by spaces to count words!

variants.title: Level 4: Form with Multiple Validation Rules
variants.description: Create a registration form with email, phone, and age validation!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use regex for validation. Email: .contains("@") && .contains("."), Phone: matches("\\d{10,11}"), Age: parse and check range!

variants.title: Level 4: Timer Application with Start/Stop/Reset
variants.description: Create a stopwatch timer with control buttons!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: timer = new Timer(1000, e -> { seconds++; update display }); Use String.format("%02d:%02d", min, sec)!

variants.title: Level 5: Complete To-Do List Application
variants.description: Build a full-featured to-do list with add, remove, edit, and mark complete!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use DefaultListModel for list operations. Add KeyListener for Enter key. Show JOptionPane for edit dialog!

variants.title: Level 5: Paint Application with Tools
variants.description: Create a drawing app with color picker, brush sizes, and clear button!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Store each stroke as ArrayList of Points. Save stroke and its color. Draw all strokes in paintComponent()!

variants.title: Level 5: Contact Manager Application
variants.description: Build a contact manager with add, search, edit, and delete features!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Validate all fields before adding. Format: name + " - " + phone + " - " + email. Use MouseListener for double-click!

variants.title: Level 5: Scientific Calculator
variants.description: Build a calculator with basic and scientific functions (sin, cos, sqrt, power)!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use Math class for scientific functions: Math.sin(), Math.cos(), Math.sqrt(), Math.pow(). Handle operator precedence!

variants.title: Level 5: Multi-Tab Note Taking App
variants.description: Create a note-taking app with multiple tabs, save and load functionality!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use JTabbedPane.addTab() and removeTabAt(). Get active tab content with getSelectedComponent(). Use FileWriter/Reader!

44.name: Event Handling in AWT and Swing

variants.title: Level 1: Simple ActionListener
variants.description: Learn to handle button clicks using ActionListener interface!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: button.addActionListener(new ActionListener() { public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { label.setText("Status: Clicked!"); } });

variants.title: Level 1: MouseListener Basics
variants.description: Handle mouse click events using MouseListener interface!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use MouseAdapter to implement only mouseClicked: panel.addMouseListener(new MouseAdapter() { public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) { ... } });

variants.title: Level 1: KeyListener Introduction
variants.description: Detect keyboard input using KeyListener interface!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use KeyAdapter: textField.addKeyListener(new KeyAdapter() { public void keyTyped(KeyEvent e) { label.setText("Key typed: " + e.getKeyChar()); } });

variants.title: Level 1: WindowListener Basics
variants.description: Handle window events like opening and closing!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use WindowAdapter: frame.addWindowListener(new WindowAdapter() { public void windowClosing(WindowEvent e) { int result = JOptionPane.showConfirmDialog(...); if (result == JOptionPane.YES_OPTION) System.exit(0); } });

variants.title: Level 1: ItemListener for CheckBox
variants.description: Detect checkbox state changes using ItemListener!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: checkbox.addItemListener(e -> { if (checkbox.isSelected()) label.setText("Status: Enabled"); else label.setText("Status: Disabled"); });

variants.title: Level 2: Mouse Pressed and Released
variants.description: Differentiate between mouse press and release events!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use MouseAdapter and override both mousePressed and mouseReleased methods to change panel background!

variants.title: Level 2: Mouse Coordinates Display
variants.description: Show mouse position when clicked!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: In mouseClicked: int x = e.getX(); int y = e.getY(); label.setText("Clicked at X: " + x + ", Y: " + y);

variants.title: Level 2: Key Code Detection
variants.description: Detect which key was pressed using key codes!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use e.getKeyCode() to get numeric code. Use KeyEvent.getKeyText(e.getKeyCode()) to get readable key name!

variants.title: Level 2: Multiple Button Handlers
variants.description: Handle multiple buttons with separate ActionListeners!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Each button needs its own ActionListener. Use lambda: btn1.addActionListener(e -> label.setBackground(Color.RED));

variants.title: Level 2: ComboBox Selection Listener
variants.description: Respond to ComboBox selection changes!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: combo.addActionListener(e -> { String color = (String)combo.getSelectedItem(); if (color.equals("Red")) label.setBackground(Color.RED); ... });

variants.title: Level 3: Mouse Dragged Event
variants.description: Track mouse dragging and draw points!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: addMouseMotionListener(new MouseMotionAdapter() { public void mouseDragged(MouseEvent e) { points.add(e.getPoint()); repaint(); } });

variants.title: Level 3: Enter Key to Submit
variants.description: Handle Enter key press in text field!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: if (e.getKeyCode() == KeyEvent.VK_ENTER) { displayArea.append(inputField.getText() + "\n"); inputField.setText(""); }

variants.title: Level 3: Focus Change Listener
variants.description: Handle focus gained and lost events!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: field1.addFocusListener(new FocusAdapter() { public void focusGained(FocusEvent e) { field1.setBackground(Color.YELLOW); } public void focusLost(FocusEvent e) { field1.setBackground(Color.WHITE); } });

variants.title: Level 3: Mouse Button Detection
variants.description: Detect which mouse button was clicked (left, right, middle)!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: if (e.getButton() == MouseEvent.BUTTON1) label.setText("Left button"); else if (e.getButton() == MouseEvent.BUTTON3) label.setText("Right button");

variants.title: Level 3: Window State Listener
variants.description: Detect window minimize and restore events!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: frame.addWindowStateListener(e -> { if ((e.getNewState() & Frame.ICONIFIED) == Frame.ICONIFIED) label.setText("Window Minimized"); else label.setText("Window Restored"); });

variants.title: Level 4: Simple Drawing Application
variants.description: Create a basic drawing app with mouse drag!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: mousePressed: start new ArrayList. mouseDragged: add points. mouseReleased: save to strokes. paintComponent: loop through strokes and draw lines!

variants.title: Level 4: Keyboard Shortcut Handler
variants.description: Implement Ctrl+S to save and Ctrl+O to open!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: if (e.isControlDown() && e.getKeyCode() == KeyEvent.VK_S) { statusLabel.setText("File saved"); }

variants.title: Level 4: Component Event Listener
variants.description: Handle component resize and move events!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: frame.addComponentListener(new ComponentAdapter() { public void componentResized(ComponentEvent e) { label.setText("Size: " + frame.getWidth() + "x" + frame.getHeight()); } });

variants.title: Level 4: Double Click Detection
variants.description: Detect double-click events on components!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: if (e.getClickCount() == 2) { panel.setBackground(new Color((int)(Math.random()*256), (int)(Math.random()*256), (int)(Math.random()*256))); }

variants.title: Level 4: Multi-Event Handler
variants.description: Handle multiple event types on same component!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Same component can have multiple listeners! Add both ActionListener for clicks and MouseListener for hover effects!

variants.title: Level 5: Complete Event Logger
variants.description: Create an application that logs all types of events!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Create a log method that appends to logArea: logArea.append(++eventCount + ". " + eventType + " at " + System.currentTimeMillis() + "\n");

variants.title: Level 5: Interactive Game Panel
variants.description: Create a simple game that responds to keyboard and mouse!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Arrow keys: if (e.getKeyCode() == KeyEvent.VK_LEFT) playerX -= 10; Mouse: playerX = e.getX(); playerY = e.getY(); Don't forget repaint()!

variants.title: Level 5: Custom Context Menu
variants.description: Create right-click context menu with event handling!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: MouseListener: if (e.isPopupTrigger() || e.getButton() == MouseEvent.BUTTON3) popup.show(textArea, e.getX(), e.getY());

variants.title: Level 5: Drag and Drop Interface
variants.description: Create draggable components within a panel!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Store mouse offset in mousePressed. In mouseDragged, calculate new position relative to parent and use setLocation()!

variants.title: Level 5: Multi-Window Event System
variants.description: Create multiple windows that communicate via events!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Store reference to main window's label in static variable. Child window can update it through ActionListener and WindowListener!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 1: Lambda Expression for Action Event
variants.description: Use lambda expression to handle button click events more concisely!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Lambda syntax: button.addActionListener(e -> label.setText("Button clicked!"));

variants.title: Intermediate Level 1: Mouse Motion Tracker with Lambda
variants.description: Track mouse movement using lambda expressions and display coordinates!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use MouseMotionAdapter and override mouseMoved: panel.addMouseMotionListener(new MouseMotionAdapter() { public void mouseMoved(MouseEvent e) { ... } });

variants.title: Intermediate Level 1: Multiple Lambda Event Handlers
variants.description: Create multiple buttons with lambda expressions for different actions!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Each button gets its own lambda: redBtn.addActionListener(e -> display.setBackground(Color.RED));

variants.title: Intermediate Level 1: Key Event with Lambda
variants.description: Handle keyboard events using modern lambda syntax!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use KeyAdapter: textArea.addKeyListener(new KeyAdapter() { public void keyPressed(KeyEvent e) { ... } });

variants.title: Intermediate Level 1: Focus Event with Lambda
variants.description: Use lambda-style event handling for focus events on text fields!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use FocusAdapter: usernameField.addFocusListener(new FocusAdapter() { public void focusGained(FocusEvent e) { ... } });

variants.title: Intermediate Level 2: Mouse Entered/Exited with Counters
variants.description: Track how many times mouse enters and exits a component!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use MouseAdapter. In mouseEntered: enterCount++; In mouseExited: exitCount++; Update label with both counts!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 2: Keyboard Combo Detection
variants.description: Detect when Ctrl+S or Ctrl+O key combinations are pressed!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: if (e.isControlDown() && e.getKeyCode() == KeyEvent.VK_S) { statusLabel.setText("Saved!"); }

variants.title: Intermediate Level 2: Mouse Button Detection
variants.description: Detect which mouse button was clicked (left, right, or middle)!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: int button = e.getButton(); if (button == MouseEvent.BUTTON1) { ... } else if (button == MouseEvent.BUTTON3) { ... }

variants.title: Intermediate Level 2: Click Count Detector
variants.description: Detect single-click vs double-click on a component!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: int clicks = e.getClickCount(); if (clicks == 1) { ... } else if (clicks == 2) { ... }

variants.title: Intermediate Level 2: Window State Listener
variants.description: Listen to window state changes (minimized, maximized, restored)!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: frame.addWindowStateListener(e -> { int state = e.getNewState(); if ((state & JFrame.ICONIFIED) != 0) { ... } });

variants.title: Intermediate Level 3: Simple Drawing App with Events
variants.description: Create a drawing application using mouse drag events!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Store points in ArrayList. On mouseDragged, add point and repaint. In paintComponent, draw all points!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 3: Event Propagation Demo
variants.description: Understand how events bubble through component hierarchy!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Add MouseListener to each component in the hierarchy. See how events flow from innermost to outermost!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 3: Custom Event Handling Class
variants.description: Create a reusable event handler class!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Create class that implements both interfaces. One handler can be reused across multiple components!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 3: Drag and Drop Basics
variants.description: Implement simple drag and drop functionality!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Store mouse offset on press. During drag, calculate new position relative to mouse and panel location!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 3: Keyboard Navigation System
variants.description: Navigate through components using Tab and arrow keys!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use 2D array to store buttons. On arrow key, calculate new row/col, check if valid, then call requestFocusInWindow()!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 4: Multi-Button Event Handler
variants.description: Handle multiple buttons with single ActionListener using getSource()!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use e.getSource() to identify which button fired the event. Cast to JButton to access button properties!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 4: Timer-Based Event System
variants.description: Use javax.swing.Timer for periodic events!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: timer = new Timer(1000, e -> { seconds++; timeLabel.setText("Time: " + seconds + " seconds"); }); Creates periodic event!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 4: Event Queue and EDT Understanding
variants.description: Understand Event Dispatch Thread and SwingUtilities!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Always create/update Swing components on EDT using SwingUtilities.invokeLater()! Long tasks should run in background thread!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 4: Input Validation with Events
variants.description: Validate user input in real-time using events!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Use keyReleased to validate after each keystroke. Show error messages in real-time. Enable submit only when all valid!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 4: Custom Mouse Cursor Events
variants.description: Change mouse cursor based on component state!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: button.setCursor(new Cursor(Cursor.HAND_CURSOR)); changes cursor when hovering over component!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 5: Complete Drawing Application
variants.description: Build full-featured paint app with multiple tools!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Store strokes and colors in parallel ArrayLists. Clear removes all, undo removes last stroke. Repaint after changes!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 5: Complete Event Logger System
variants.description: Build comprehensive event monitoring system!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Create logEvent method that formats and appends to log. Add listeners to all components and log every event type!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 5: Interactive Game with Events
variants.description: Create simple game using keyboard and mouse events!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Move player with arrow keys or mouse clicks. Check distance to target. If close enough, increment score and spawn new target!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 5: Multi-Window Communication System
variants.description: Create system where multiple windows communicate via events!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Store all windows in ArrayList. When event occurs in one window, update main log and notify other windows!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 5: Complete Form with Validation
variants.description: Build production-ready form with comprehensive event handling!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Validate on keyReleased. Use regex for email/phone. Update status labels (green ✓ or red ✗). Enable submit only when all valid!

variants.title: Level 1: Create a Simple ComboBox
variants.description: Create a dropdown list (ComboBox) for selecting courses!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Create with JComboBox<String> combo = new JComboBox<>(courses); Then add to frame!

variants.title: Level 1: Create a Slider
variants.description: Create a slider to select values from 0 to 100!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: JSlider slider = new JSlider(0, 100, 50); Sets min, max, and initial value!

variants.title: Level 2: ComboBox with Action Listener
variants.description: Display the selected item from ComboBox in a label!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: combo.addActionListener(e -> display.setText("Selected: " + combo.getSelectedItem())); Shows selection!

variants.title: Level 2: Slider Value Display
variants.description: Show the current slider value in real-time!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: slider.addChangeListener(e -> valueLabel.setText("Value: " + slider.getValue())); Updates in real-time!

variants.title: Beginner Level 2 Activity 3: Slider controls
variants.description: Practice Slider controls for level 2!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Focus on implementing Slider controls step by step!

variants.title: Level 3: Simple JList with Items
variants.description: Create a list component to display multiple items!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: JList<String> list = new JList<>(students); JScrollPane scroll = new JScrollPane(list); frame.add(scroll);

variants.title: Level 3: JList with Selection Listener
variants.description: Detect which item is selected in the list!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: list.addListSelectionListener(e -> { if (!e.getValueIsAdjusting()) display.setText("You selected: " + list.getSelectedValue()); });

variants.title: Level 3: Basic JTable Display
variants.description: Create a simple table with rows and columns!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: JTable table = new JTable(data, columns); Always wrap JTable in JScrollPane for headers!

variants.title: Level 3: JSpinner with Custom Model
variants.description: Create a spinner with custom number ranges!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: SpinnerNumberModel model = new SpinnerNumberModel(75, 60, 100, 1); Restricts input to valid grade range!

variants.title: Level 3: JTextArea with Scroll
variants.description: Create a multi-line text area with scrollbars!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: JTextArea area = new JTextArea(10, 30); Always wrap in JScrollPane for automatic scrollbars!

variants.title: Level 4: JTable with Selection
variants.description: Get selected row data from a table!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: int row = table.getSelectedRow(); String name = (String) table.getValueAt(row, 1); Gets data from selected row!

variants.title: Level 4: DefaultListModel for Dynamic List
variants.description: Add and remove items from a list dynamically!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: listModel.addElement(inputField.getText()); and listModel.remove(list.getSelectedIndex()); for dynamic list operations!

variants.title: Level 4: JComboBox with Custom Items
variants.description: Add and remove items from ComboBox dynamically!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: comboModel.addElement(inputField.getText()); and comboModel.removeElement(combo.getSelectedItem()); for dynamic combo!

variants.title: Level 4: JSlider with Labels
variants.description: Create a slider with custom labels at specific positions!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Hashtable<Integer, JLabel> labels = new Hashtable<>(); Use put() to add labels at specific values!

variants.title: Level 4: JProgressBar with Manual Update
variants.description: Create a progress bar that updates based on button clicks!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: int current = progressBar.getValue() + 1; progressBar.setValue(current); Updates progress manually!

variants.title: Level 5: Complete Todo List App
variants.description: Build a todo list with add, remove, and clear all features!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: listModel.addElement(taskField.getText()); Use listModel.getSize() to get count. listModel.clear() removes all!

variants.title: Level 5: Student Data Manager
variants.description: Create app to add and display student records in a table!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: tableModel.addRow(new Object[]{id, name, course, year}); Use table.getSelectedRow() to get selected index!

variants.title: Level 5: Multi-Tab Application
variants.description: Create an app with multiple tabs for different features!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Create separate JPanels for each tab with different components. tabbedPane.addTab("Title", panel) adds each tab!

variants.title: Level 5: Interactive Component Dashboard
variants.description: Build a dashboard with multiple interactive components!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Combine multiple components! Add listeners to each to update status and progress bar!

variants.title: Level 5: Complete Contact List App
variants.description: Build a contact manager with JList and multiple operations!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: String contact = nameField.getText() + " - " + phoneField.getText(); For edit, use setElementAt(value, index)!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 1: JTable with Custom Model
variants.description: Create a JTable with DefaultTableModel for dynamic data!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: model.addRow(new Object[]{"Name", 25, "City"}); adds a row dynamically!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 1: JTree for Hierarchical Data
variants.description: Create a tree structure to show organizational hierarchy!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Create root node, add children with node.add(childNode), then new JTree(root)!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 1: JList with Selection Listener
variants.description: Create a list that responds to selections!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: list.addListSelectionListener(e -> { if (!e.getValueIsAdjusting()) { display.setText(list.getSelectedValue()); } });

variants.title: Intermediate Level 1: JProgressBar with Timer
variants.description: Create an animated progress bar using Timer!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Timer timer = new Timer(100, e -> { int val = progressBar.getValue() + 1; progressBar.setValue(val); if (val == 100) ((Timer)e.getSource()).stop(); });

variants.title: Intermediate Level 1: JTabbedPane with Multiple Tabs
variants.description: Create a tabbed interface with different content!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Create panels, add content, then tabbedPane.addTab("Title", panel); for each tab!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 2: Editable JTable
variants.description: Make table cells editable and handle cell value changes!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: model.addTableModelListener(e -> { int row = e.getFirstRow(); int col = e.getColumn(); ... });

variants.title: Intermediate Level 2: JTree Selection Handler
variants.description: Handle tree node selection and display node information!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: tree.addTreeSelectionListener(e -> { DefaultMutableTreeNode node = (DefaultMutableTreeNode)tree.getLastSelectedPathComponent(); display.setText(node.toString()); });

variants.title: Intermediate Level 2: Custom Cell Renderer for JList
variants.description: Create a custom renderer to display list items with colors!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Extend DefaultListCellRenderer, override getListCellRendererComponent, customize appearance, then list.setCellRenderer(new CustomRenderer())!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 2: JSpinner with Custom Model
variants.description: Create a spinner with custom list of values!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: SpinnerListModel model = new SpinnerListModel(Arrays.asList(values)); Then new JSpinner(model)!

variants.title: Intermediate Level 2: JComboBox with Custom Objects
variants.description: Create a ComboBox with custom objects instead of strings!
variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Create a class, override toString() for display, then JComboBox<Student> combo = new JComboBox<>(studentArray)!

variants.hint: 💡 Hint: Create panels with different layouts, add components to each, then add panels to frame!
